caveats: a: OEWS publishes the needed mix at parent NAICS 238300 and excludes self-employed workers.
a: Task exposure is a bounded translation from occupational duties, not a measured displacement share.
a: Computer vision and measurement tools may expand exposure, but unstructured job sites constrain substitution of installation labor.
rho: BTOS covers all nonfarm sectors and asks whether any business function used AI, not how much labor opportunity was implemented.
rho: Small flooring contractors may have weaker data integration and training capacity than the national business population.
rho: The range excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
e: No source directly measures eligibility under the frozen lens.
e: The supplied count is an estimate created from broader datasets and margin assumptions.
e: Project-based revenue can still meet the repeat-outsourcing lens, but repeat-customer intensity varies materially by residential, commercial, and channel mix.
s5: The Census owner-age figure covers responding owners across employer industries and has a 2018 reference year.
s5: BizBuySell transactions are voluntarily reported, span broad building and construction categories, and skew toward marketable firms.
s5: Owner retirement intent does not imply a completed qualifying transfer.
q: The source population is residential builders and remodelers using one software platform, not a representative flooring-contractor sample.
q: Retention varies with residential versus commercial mix, subcontractor channel, backlog, local competition, and whether customers can observe the cost change.
q: This estimate addresses benefit sharing and repricing only, not implementation difficulty or demand loss.
d5: The BLS occupational group includes tile and stone setters, while tile and stone contracting is classified outside NAICS 238330.
d5: Employment is affected by productivity and labor supply and is not a direct quantity-demand measure.
d5: Construction demand is cyclical and differs sharply across carpet, resilient flooring, hardwood, residential, and commercial work.
o: Current task content is not a forecast.
o: Click-lock products and retailer-led installation can shift some demand away from standalone contractors.
o: The estimate concerns continued need for an accountable operator, not the amount of human labor inside that operator.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project revenue is earned through competitively quoted lump-sum or unit-price installation, replacement, repair, and refinishing work. Individual projects are episodic, but the service recurs through flooring wear, tenant turnover, remodeling, new construction, and repeat relationships with general contractors, retailers, property managers, and owners.
lens.excluded_activities: Retail-led sale and installation classified outside NAICS 238330; ceramic tile, stone, terrazzo, concrete, and epoxy-flooring trades classified elsewhere; captive internal crews; shell entities; non-control financing vehicles; and operations outside the stated EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors installing, replacing, sanding, finishing, and refinishing resilient flooring, carpet, linoleum, and hardwood for property owners, general contractors, retailers, property managers, and other external customers, including residential and commercial new work, alterations, maintenance, and repair.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation and wage mix for parent industry NAICS 238300, not 238330, while O*NET describes tasks for floor layers rather than measuring AI substitution. I grouped management, business, sales, office, field-supervision, and installation work, assigned bounded task exposure to estimating, takeoff, scheduling, dispatch, documentation, customer communication, billing, bookkeeping, and marketing, and weighted those judgments by the BLS employment shares and mean wages. Parent-industry inclusion of drywall, painting, and other finish trades may bias the estimate either way; assigning only a small exposed share to manual installation is intended to limit upward bias.
caveats: OEWS publishes the needed mix at parent NAICS 238300 and excludes self-employed workers.
Task exposure is a bounded translation from occupational duties, not a measured displacement share.
Computer vision and measurement tools may expand exposure, but unstructured job sites constrain substitution of installation labor.
a.change_evidence: Obtain payroll dollars and time-on-task by occupation from a representative sample of NAICS 238330 firms, then run task-level workflow studies for estimating, measurement and takeoff, scheduling, dispatch, purchasing, customer service, billing, and field documentation, recording which tasks are already automated.
a.rationale: In BLS's parent-industry data, construction and extraction occupations are 70.96% of employment, while management is 5.32%, business and financial operations 4.63%, sales 2.94%, and office support 8.59%. O*NET shows that core floor-layer work is site-bound preparation, cutting, inspection, fitting, adhesion, and finishing, but also identifies estimating and project-management software. The resulting wage-weighted opportunity is concentrated in overhead and field coordination rather than physical installation.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for flooring installers and tile and stone setters, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 238330's service basket. I convert its 6% ten-year employment growth to an approximately 3% five-year increase and widen the bounds for productivity, construction cycles, material substitution, and the inclusion of tile occupations outside 238330. Employment can understate demand if productivity rises and can overstate it if labor intensity rises.
caveats: The BLS occupational group includes tile and stone setters, while tile and stone contracting is classified outside NAICS 238330.
Employment is affected by productivity and labor supply and is not a direct quantity-demand measure.
Construction demand is cyclical and differs sharply across carpet, resilient flooring, hardwood, residential, and commercial work.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain a five-year constant-dollar forecast for installed flooring square footage by product and end market from Catalina Research, FMI, or Freedonia, and reconcile it with Census construction put-in-place, housing completions, remodeling expenditure, and commercial renovation forecasts.
d5.rationale: BLS expects overall flooring-installer and tile-setter employment to grow 6% from 2024 to 2034 and identifies new homes and renovation as the primary demand sources. Its detailed outlook is mixed, with carpet installers declining while resilient-flooring layers and tile setters grow, supporting a near-flat to modestly growing service basket rather than a uniform expansion.

caveats: No source directly measures eligibility under the frozen lens.
The supplied count is an estimate created from broader datasets and margin assumptions.
Project-based revenue can still meet the repeat-outsourcing lens, but repeat-customer intensity varies materially by residential, commercial, and channel mix.
e.change_evidence: Draw a stratified sample from the NAICS 238330 business register and verify revenue source, customer concentration, owner labor, subcontractor dependence, normalized EBITDA, standalone systems, and correct NAICS classification using tax returns and job-level ledgers.
e.rationale: The frozen firm count is already bridged into the stated EBITDA band. Census defines 238330 around externally contracted installation, replacement, maintenance, repair, and refinishing, and separates retail-led installation and adjacent tile, concrete, and epoxy trades. Most band-qualified firms should therefore fit the lens; the deduction covers miscoding, owner-dependent practices without transferable systems, captive-like arrangements, and firms whose normalized economics fall outside the band on diligence.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23833 ($5,344,278,728) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23833 ($25,367,089,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23833. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $295,602 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238330); class <5: 13,771 firms, avg revenue/firm $336,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $26,763 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,253 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,595,011 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $126,963 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,058 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,626,822 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $288,695 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 615 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,955,252 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $951,638 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 70 firms, avg revenue/firm $64,233,743 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,113,006 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 10 firms, avg revenue/firm $145,738,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,600,793 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 70

o.bridge: O*NET and BLS describe current occupation duties and physical requirements, not the year-five share of service quantity still requiring an accountable flooring operator. I infer persistence from site-specific inspection, substrate preparation, measuring, cutting, fitting, adhesion, finishing, and customer-facing quality responsibility, with a deduction for do-it-yourself products, prefabrication, retailer coordination, and limited robotic assistance. Current-duty evidence may overstate persistence if field robotics advances unexpectedly, but software alone cannot perform the documented physical tasks.
caveats: Current task content is not a forecast.
Click-lock products and retailer-led installation can shift some demand away from standalone contractors.
The estimate concerns continued need for an accountable operator, not the amount of human labor inside that operator.
o.change_evidence: Track the share of installed square footage completed by do-it-yourself customers, retailer-managed gig crews, prefabricated systems, and robotic installation; interview major flooring manufacturers, installation networks, insurers, and large contractors about five-year commercialization and warranty accountability.
o.rationale: The present service basket is dominated by physical work in varied occupied and construction environments. O*NET reports that 89% of floor layers use their hands continually or almost continually, and BLS describes preparation, cutting, placement, finishing, stamina, strength, and visual-quality requirements. AI can change coordination and planning without removing the need for an accountable contractor on most outsourced jobs.

q.bridge: CoConstruct measures contract choice and project margins among residential builders and remodelers using its software, not flooring contractors' five-year retention of AI benefits. I use its finding that fixed-price contracts were used 80% of the time and the contractual distinction between fixed price and open book to infer strong in-project retention, then discount for competitive rebidding, unit-price resets, general-contractor negotiation, customer sharing, and renewal repricing. The builder sample may overstate both fixed-price prevalence and retained benefit for commercial flooring subcontractors.
caveats: The source population is residential builders and remodelers using one software platform, not a representative flooring-contractor sample.
Retention varies with residential versus commercial mix, subcontractor channel, backlog, local competition, and whether customers can observe the cost change.
This estimate addresses benefit sharing and repricing only, not implementation difficulty or demand loss.
q.change_evidence: Collect contract-type and bid-history data from LMM flooring firms, then compare realized gross margin, win rate, price revisions, and renewal pricing before and after specific AI workflow deployments by customer channel and contract type.
q.rationale: A contractor can retain cost savings on an already signed lump-sum job, but flooring work is frequently rebid project by project and buyers compare unit prices. Benefits that improve response time, accuracy, close rates, or capacity may be retained longer than visible labor-rate savings, while recurring commercial buyers and general contractors are likely to capture part of the gain at later bids.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures recent AI use across all nonfarm businesses and firm sizes, not the five-year implemented share of exposed tasks in flooring contractors. I use its 19.8% May 2026 all-business adoption reading and the finding that usage remained below 20% among the smallest firms as a starting point, then allow five years for diffusion while discounting for fragmented systems, variable job-site data, review requirements, and small-firm change capacity. Cross-industry adoption likely overstates construction readiness, while rapid improvement in estimating and office tools could make the base conservative.
caveats: BTOS covers all nonfarm sectors and asks whether any business function used AI, not how much labor opportunity was implemented.
Small flooring contractors may have weaker data integration and training capacity than the national business population.
The range excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Survey at least 30 LMM flooring contractors annually on production use, workflow penetration, exception rates, avoided hires, error costs, and staff adoption; corroborate with anonymized usage telemetry from flooring estimating, CRM, scheduling, and accounting vendors.
rho.rationale: Half of the identified opportunity is expected to become operational because common office workflows can use packaged AI without changing installation methods, but estimate accuracy, material ordering, customer commitments, safety, quality control, and field exceptions still require accountable review. Current small-firm adoption is meaningful but far from universal.

s5.bridge: Census observed that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were at least 55 in 2018, while BizBuySell documents actual construction-business sales but not the population denominator or flooring-only transfer rate. I translate broad aging pressure and visible construction deal flow into a bounded five-year control-transfer probability, excluding closures, inherited continuity without a control event, and internal reorganizations. Marketplace transactions select for sale-ready firms and bias upward; the old all-industry age distribution and unobserved off-market transfers can bias in either direction.
caveats: The Census owner-age figure covers responding owners across employer industries and has a 2018 reference year.
BizBuySell transactions are voluntarily reported, span broad building and construction categories, and skew toward marketable firms.
Owner retirement intent does not imply a completed qualifying transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Match five years of Secretary of State control changes, SBA acquisition-loan records, broker closing data, and PitchBook or GF Data transactions to a denominator of LMM NAICS 238330 firms; interview owners about exit timing, successor readiness, and willingness to sell control.
s5.rationale: A substantial aging-owner cohort supports transfer supply, and BizBuySell analyzed 3,142 reported sold construction businesses with a median 207 days on market, confirming that the category can transact. However, many owner exits end in closure or family continuity, and many flooring firms depend heavily on the owner, so only a minority of eligible firms are expected to complete a qualifying control transfer within five years.

principal_driver: The principal driver is the ability to automate estimating and project administration around a durable, locally delivered physical service.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is that hands-on installation dominates employment while transfer and benefit-retention evidence is indirect.

caveats: S1's JavaScript-only audit response prevented direct verification of the detailed NAICS inclusion and exclusion wording; this does not change the ESTIMATE classification of e, and a correction would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The a and rho bases remain judgmental transformations from a parent-industry occupation mix, one floor-layer occupation, and cross-industry AI adoption; the bridges disclose the population and construct gaps, but no firm-level flooring workflow evidence anchors the selected bases.

The q source supports residential-builder contract prevalence and terminology, not a five-year retention rate for AI benefits at flooring contractors; the selected q range is therefore substantially model-conditioned despite its honest PROXY label.

The s5 estimate combines dated all-industry owner-age evidence with selected marketplace transactions that have no flooring-specific population denominator, so it demonstrates aging pressure and transaction feasibility rather than a five-year control-transfer probability.

The d5 and o bridges convert occupational projections and current physical duties into service-quantity and operator-persistence estimates; inclusion of tile and stone occupations outside NAICS 238330 and the absence of direct quantity forecasts limit precision.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census profile resolves to the NAICS 238330 Flooring Contractors page, but the reopened response is JavaScript-only, so the detailed inclusion and exclusion language could not be text-verified from the rendered audit response. The primitive is already labeled ESTIMATE and does not claim the page measures eligibility.

The May 2023 OEWS page is explicitly for parent NAICS 238300 and reports the cited occupational shares, including construction and extraction at 70.96%, management at 5.32%, business and financial operations at 4.63%, and office support at 8.59%.

O*NET documents the cited preparation, cutting, fitting, adhesion, and finishing tasks, reports 89% continual or near-continual hand use, and lists flooring estimating and project-management software. It supports the task facts, not a measured AI-exposure or five-year persistence share, as the bridges acknowledge.

The Census BTOS article reports a 19.8% national AI-use rate as of May 3, 2026, less than 20% use among firms with four or fewer employees, and the stated all-nonfarm, any-business-function survey scope.

The CoConstruct article states that builders used fixed-price contracts 80% of the time, analyzes more than 60,000 U.S. residential projects from 2018 through 2020, and distinguishes fixed-price from open-book contracts. It does not directly measure flooring-contractor AI-benefit retention, a limitation explicitly disclosed in q.

The Census infographic reports 51% age 55 or older and identifies the 2019 Annual Business Survey, data year 2018; it also states that estimates cover 4.1 million responding owners of employer businesses rather than all U.S. owners.

BizBuySell reports 3,142 sold listings analyzed, a median 207 days on market, inclusion of flooring among represented construction specialties, and that transaction data are based on businesses sold on or reported to the marketplace.

BLS describes the cited physical duties, projects 6% aggregate employment growth from 2024 to 2034, identifies new homes and renovation as primary demand sources, and reports divergent outlooks for carpet installers, resilient floor layers, and tile and stone setters.

sources.supports: Defines NAICS 238330 as installation of resilient floor tile, carpeting, linoleum, and hardwood, including new work, alterations, maintenance, and repairs; lists adjacent and retail-led activities classified elsewhere.

sources.supports: Reports parent-industry employment and wages, including construction and extraction at 70.96% of employment, management at 5.32%, business and financial operations at 4.63%, sales at 2.94%, and office support at 8.59%.

sources.supports: Reports nationally representative BTOS evidence: 19.8% of businesses used AI as of May 3, 2026, adoption rose with firm size, and fewer than 20% of firms with at most four employees reported use.
sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users

sources.title: Fixed Price Projects Have 28% Higher Average Profit Margins

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were at least age 55 in the 2019 Annual Business Survey, with data year 2018 and stated population limitations.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Reports 3,142 building and construction businesses sold on or reported to BizBuySell, a median 207 days on market, and transaction observations that include flooring among the represented specialty services.
sources.title: Construction Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks

sources.supports: Describes physical duties and requirements, projects 6% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, identifies new homes and renovation as primary demand sources, and reports divergent outlooks across carpet, resilient flooring, and tile occupations.
sources.title: Flooring Installers and Tile and Stone Setters

title: Flooring Contractors
